question: Which HTML element defines the page title?
explanation: The <title> element, placed inside <head>, defines the page title.

question: Where does the page title appear in a browser?
options: In the body of the page
explanation: The title appears in the browser tab, search results, bookmarks, and browser history.

question: Is the <title> element required in HTML?
options: Yes, it's required for valid HTML
explanation: The <title> element is required for valid HTML documents. HTML validators will flag missing titles.

question: What is the recommended character length for SEO titles?
explanation: Google typically displays 50-60 characters (about 600px). Longer titles get truncated with an ellipsis.

question: Where must the <title> element be placed in an HTML document?
explanation: The <title> element must be placed inside the <head> section of the HTML document.

question: How do you change the page title dynamically with JavaScript?
explanation: document.title = 'New Title' changes the page title dynamically using JavaScript.

question: What happens when a page doesn't have a <title> element?
options: The browser shows 'Untitled' or the filename
The page redirects to the homepage
explanation: Without a <title>, browsers typically display 'Untitled' or the page's filename in the tab.

question: Which of these is a well-written page title?
options: How to Center a Div — CSS Tutorial | SiteName
explanation: A good title is descriptive, includes keywords, and mentions the brand. 'How to Center a Div — CSS Tutorial | SiteName' follows best practices.

question: What is keyword stuffing in titles?
explanation: Keyword stuffing is the practice of cramming excessive keywords into a title, which search engines penalize.

question: What should the title of a 404 error page be?
explanation: A good 404 title clearly indicates the error while maintaining brand presence: '404: Page Not Found — SiteName'.

question: Can you use HTML tags inside the <title> element?
options: No, only text content is allowed
explanation: The <title> element can only contain plain text. HTML tags inside it will be displayed as literal text.

question: What is the best title format for a blog article?
options: Just the article title without brand
explanation: Article Title — Site Name places the specific content first (important for SEO) and the brand last.

question: How do search engines use page titles?
options: Titles are the clickable headline in search results
explanation: Search engines display the page title as the clickable headline in search results. It's a key ranking factor.

question: Why should each page have a unique title?
options: It makes the site load faster
explanation: Unique titles help search engines understand page differentiation and help users identify which page they're on.

question: What is a 'title separator' commonly used between content and brand?
options: All of the above are acceptable
explanation: Em dashes, pipes, and hyphens are all acceptable title separators. The key is consistency across the site.

question: What is the first thing a screen reader announces when loading a page?
explanation: Screen readers announce the page title first, making it critical for accessibility.

question: If your site is called 'MySite' and the article is '10 CSS Tips', what is the best title?
explanation: The article title should come first for SEO, with the brand at the end: '10 CSS Tips — MySite'.

question: What happens if a title is longer than 60 characters in Google search results?
options: It gets truncated with an ellipsis
explanation: Google truncates titles that are longer than ~60 characters (600px), adding an ellipsis at the end.

question: How can you update a title when a user receives a new message in a web app?
options: Use document.title to set a notification count
explanation: document.title = '(3) New Messages' dynamically updates the title to show notifications.

question: What should you avoid in page titles?
explanation: Excessive special characters can cause display issues in browser tabs and search results.
